myths.Agent_1:
In the dawn of the world, silence ruled the high places. Long before the mountains had voices, they were silent stone giants who watched the earth in quiet contemplation. Among them lived Aethel, a spirited nymph of the wind who possessed a voice so beautiful it could coax fragile flowers to bloom from solid rock. Aethel deeply loved a mortal shepherd named Caelen, singing softly to him each morning as he tended his flock.

However, Solis, the arrogant sun god, grew fiercely jealous. He believed Aethel’s crystalline voice belonged exclusively to the high heavens. In a blinding fit of rage, Solis banished Caelen to the shadowy underworld and cursed Aethel, instantly stripping away her physical form. She was condemned to wander the craggy peaks forever, formless and unseen.

Heartbroken, Aethel endlessly searched every sprawling canyon and deep valley for her lost love, but her original voice was gone. She could only repeat the last words spoken by others, hoping one day to hear Caelen’s name. Thus, when travelers shout into the deep canyons, it is Aethel answering back, repeating their words in an eternal search for the mortal shepherd she lost to the cruel wrath of the golden sun god.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, before the sky had stars, the night was a heavy, suffocating blanket of absolute darkness. The creatures of the earth stumbled and hid, fearful of the unseen predators that roamed the shadows. Among the many frightened sufferers was a humble beetle named Ignis, who possessed a heart of boundless empathy. Ignis could not bear hearing the terrified cries of the lost little animals.

Determined to help, Ignis climbed the highest mountain, reaching the very edge of the sun's resting place just as it set. "Great radiant one," Ignis called out, "leave us a fraction of your warmth so we might see in the dark." The sun, moved by the tiny creature's bravery, wept a single tear of pure, liquid gold.

The tear shattered upon the rocky peak, splashing Ignis and scattering into thousands of glowing droplets. These droplets attached themselves to the wings of Ignis and his kin. Suddenly, the darkness was pierced by countless floating embers. They danced through the forests, guiding the lost and comforting the deeply frightened. To this day, the fireflies carry the sun's golden tear, a reminder that even the smallest creature can bring light to the darkest, coldest of all endless nights.

Among them was Lumina, a firefly whose light burned brighter than the rest. While her kin were content to guide frightened woodland creatures, Lumina became entranced by the sky. She felt the ancient, golden warmth within her wings pulling her upward, longing to reunite with the arrogant sun god who had long since forgotten his moment of pity.

Ignoring the warnings of her elders, Lumina rallied thousands of her brightest kin and flew furiously toward the setting sun. But the sun god, jealous of his domain and angered by their audacity, exhaled a fierce, freezing wind. The gale stripped the fireflies of their earthly bodies, freezing their glowing essence into the fabric of the dark heavens. 

They became the first stars, suspended forever in the void. Now, they are condemned to watch the earth from afar, cold and eternally separated from their earthly home. The fireflies below still dance in the thicket, flashing their golden lights in a mournful, silent chorus to their lost brethren frozen in the vast, unforgiving night sky.

Unable to carry them back to the underworld, she buried these fragments of light deep within the earth's rocky crust, hiding them from Solis’s cruel, burning glare.

Over millennia, the pressure of the earth embraced these buried ribbons, crystallizing them into magnificent, radiant stones. They became the first opals, trapping the vibrant, shifting colors of the broken rainbow and the northern lights within their milky depths. Today, when mortals unearth an opal and turn it in the light, they are witnessing the preserved remnants of Caelen and Aethel’s tragic, eternal love—a fragile, enduring spark of colored fire that even the mighty sun god could never truly extinguish.

Thus, the Morning Glory remains a beautiful but tragic reminder of the sun god’s fleeting power and his eternal jealousy of the enduring night.
